aids.title: C-M-L-F: Chain Operations
aids.content: Create node (malloc), Make link (set next pointer), Locate position (traverse), Free memory (delete). C-M-L-F covers the four essential chain operations.
aids.forRemembering: The four fundamental operations on chains in C

aids.title: Train Carriages
aids.content: A linked list is like a train. Each carriage (node) holds passengers (data) and has a coupling (next pointer) to the carriage behind it. The engine is the head pointer. The last carriage has no coupling (NULL). To add a carriage, you just connect it with a coupling -- no need to rebuild the entire train.
aids.forRemembering: How nodes are connected in a chain and why insertion is efficient

aids.title: Always Check malloc Return
aids.content: malloc can return NULL if memory is exhausted. Always check: if (newNode == NULL) { printf("Memory allocation failed"); exit(1); }. This prevents segmentation faults in exam code and real programs.
aids.forRemembering: Common mistake in linked list implementation

aids.title: Delete = Bypass + Free
aids.content: To delete a node, remember two steps: Bypass (redirect predecessor's pointer) then Free (release the memory). Never free before bypass, or you lose the link to the rest of the list.
aids.forRemembering: Correct order of operations when deleting a node
